text: I can commit to July 5th.

Worth it
I see your point, but I have one concern.
I am not sure that will work, because we tried it in March.
What if we give them the reports instead?
I would suggest a two-week pilot.
The vendor starts Monday, on the integration. The client is happy. Any problem with that?
I see your point, and I understand the client. But I have one concern, and I do not want to hesitate about it.
Go ahead. But bring me facts, not feelings.
In March we added a vendor during the test phase. Integration took three extra weeks. That is my evidence.
That was a different team. Do not assume it will happen again.
Fair. But the downside is the same: two weeks of onboarding, and those weeks come out of testing.
So you want me to tell the client no?
No. I would suggest an alternative: the vendor takes the reports, my team keeps the integration. The client still sees a new team.
That might work. Convince me on Friday, with the March numbers.
The downside is two weeks of onboarding.
I do not agree, but I will support the decision.

Diogo, quick one before the meeting. I have decided to bring a new vendor into the project. They start on Monday, and they will take the integration work. I know it is late in the project, and I know your team is used to working alone. But the client is nervous, and this makes them feel safe. I am not asking for a debate in the meeting. If you have a concern, bring it to me now, with evidence, and bring me an alternative. What I will not accept is a long list of problems and no solution. And Diogo, do not go quiet in the meeting and complain later.

Sophia, I see your point, and I understand why the client feels safer. But I have one concern, and I want to be direct about it. In March we brought a vendor in during the test phase, and the integration took three extra weeks. That is my evidence. The downside here is the same: a new team needs two weeks just to understand our system, and those two weeks come out of testing. I am not saying no. I would suggest an alternative: give them the reports, not the integration. They start on Monday, the client sees a new team, and my team keeps the part that is risky. If the reports go well, we give them more in July. I think that is worth it. If you still want them on the integration, I will make it work, but I want the extra two weeks in the plan.

Sophia announces that a new vendor starts on Monday, on the integration. Diogo softens, and says he has one concern. He gives his evidence: in March, the same decision cost three extra weeks. He names the downside: two weeks of onboarding, taken out of testing. Finally, he offers an alternative: the vendor takes the reports, and his team keeps the integration.
